title: Urinary Incontinence and Pelvic Floor Screening
synonyms: Urinary Incontinence and Pelvic Floor Screening

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Urinary incontinence and pelvic floor symptoms are common and treatable. Screening in FIFOEMS should identify who is suitable for conservative pelvic health management and who needs medical or specialist referral.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Leakage with cough, lift, jump, or urgency
- Nocturia, frequency, urgency, or incomplete emptying sensation
- Heaviness, pressure, or reduced confidence with exercise and work tasks
- Postnatal or menopausal symptom onset may be reported

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Red flags include haematuria, recurrent UTI, pelvic pain, prolapse beyond expected symptoms, neurological change, urinary retention, or unexplained weight loss
- Incontinence may coexist with pelvic floor overactivity, constipation, or breathing and load-management issues
- Screening should be respectful, private, and consent-driven

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Ask whether symptoms are affecting daily function, work, exercise, and confidence
- Screen for urinary red flags, retention, infection, and major pain
- Escalate for retention, significant prolapse concern, recurrent infection, or non-benign features

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Provide education on bladder habits, pressure management, and pelvic floor training referral where appropriate
- Consider overlap with constipation, chronic cough, obesity, and lifting load
- Refer for pelvic health physio, GP, continence, or specialist review depending on severity and red flags

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Not every person volunteering leakage wants treatment, but all should know it is common and often modifiable
